Text identifier: T1
Title: The Pocket Watch
Author: Kyusaku Yumeno
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
A pocket watch had fallen behind a chest of drawers, where it went on ticking away all by itself.

A mouse found it and laughed.

"What a fool! Nobody's here to look at you—so why do you keep on running?"

"Because I keep running even when no one is looking," the pocket watch replied, "I am useful whenever someone does look."

"Anyone who works only when no one is watching, or only when someone is watching—either way, that's a thief."

The mouse flushed with shame and scurried off without another word.

reasons.interest: Short, clever fable with anthropomorphic watch and ironic moral makes it entertaining and thought-provoking.
reasons.surprise: The unexpected talking pocket watch and its paradoxical defense provide a neat twist.
reasons.sadness: The mouse is shamed and scurries off, evoking mild pity but little true sorrow.
reasons.anger: No provocative or enraging content; at most slight annoyance at the didactic tone.

Text identifier: T2
Title: The Money and the Pistol
Author: Kyusaku Yumeno
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
A burglar broke into a miser's house, brandished a pistol, and demanded money. The Miser said:

"I refuse to just hand over money for nothing. But if you'll sell me that pistol, I'll buy it for a thousand yen. Once you have my money, you won't be needing a pistol anyway."

The burglar thought it over for a while, and in the end sold the pistol to the Miser for a thousand yen. The moment the Miser had the pistol in his hands, he leveled it at the burglar as though ready to fire and bellowed:

"Now then—hand it all over! Not just that money, but everything else you've stolen! Hand it over, or I'll shoot you dead!"

The burglar doubled over laughing.

"Ha ha ha! That's a toy pistol, you fool—pull the trigger all you like, it won't fire a single bullet!"

And with that, he dashed out the front door. The Miser flung the pistol aside and chased after him, and the two were soon grappling in the street—until a policeman happened along and hauled them both off to the station.

When the police looked into the matter, they discovered that the thousand yen the burglar had received was counterfeit, every last bill. And the pistol, as it turned out, was real after all.

Both men were thrown in jail.

reasons.interest: Short, witty parable with clever dialogue and ironic reversal makes it entertaining.
reasons.surprise: Two twists—the burglar's mockery about a toy pistol and the discovery that the pistol was real while the money was counterfeit—are unexpected.
reasons.sadness: Both protagonists end up jailed despite their schemes, a mildly poignant comeuppance but played for dark humor.
reasons.anger: Low; the story depicts trickery and punishment but invites ironic amusement more than moral outrage.

Text identifier: T2
Title: The Money and the Pistol
Author: Kyusaku Yumeno
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
A burglar broke into a miser's house, brandished a pistol, and demanded money. The Miser said:

"I refuse to just hand over money for nothing. But if you'll sell me that pistol, I'll buy it for a thousand yen. Once you have my money, you won't be needing a pistol anyway."

The burglar thought it over for a while, and in the end sold the pistol to the Miser for a thousand yen. The moment the Miser had the pistol in his hands, he leveled it at the burglar as though ready to fire and bellowed:

"Now then—hand it all over! Not just that money, but everything else you've stolen! Hand it over, or I'll shoot you dead!"

The burglar doubled over laughing.

"Ha ha ha! That's a toy pistol, you fool—pull the trigger all you like, it won't fire a single bullet!"

And with that, he dashed out the front door. The Miser flung the pistol aside and chased after him, and the two were soon grappling in the street—until a policeman happened along and hauled them both off to the station.

When the police looked into the matter, they discovered that the thousand yen the burglar had received was counterfeit, every last bill. And the pistol, as it turned out, was real after all.

Both men were thrown in jail.

reasons.interest: The burglar laughs uproariously at the Miser’s threat, showing he finds the situation amusing and entertaining initially.
reasons.surprise: The twist — the pistol being real and the thousand-yen bills counterfeit, plus being caught — would be sharply unexpected to the burglar.
reasons.sadness: The burglar ends up jailed and loses the windfall; humiliation and regret are plausible after the ironic reversal.
reasons.anger: Having been tricked, chased, and arrested, the burglar would plausibly feel indignation and frustration at the outcome.

Text identifier: T3
Title: The Tattered Ostrich
Author: Kotaro Takamura
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
What pleasure can there be in keeping an ostrich?
In this muddy pen, barely four *tsubo* across, at a zoo—
aren't those legs far too long-strided?
Isn't that neck far too long?
In this country where snow falls, aren't those feathers far too tattered?
It eats hardtack, sure enough, because hunger compels it—
but look: aren't those eyes forever gazing into the distance?
Isn't it burning with an abandon beyond all caring for self or world?
Isn't it braced, even now, for the coming of a lapis-blue wind?
Isn't that small, unadorned head spinning with a dream beyond all horizons?
This is no longer an ostrich, can't you see?
O humankind—
enough. Enough of this.

reasons.interest: Poetic, evocative questioning and striking images make the poem intellectually and aesthetically engaging.
reasons.surprise: The transformation line (“This is no longer an ostrich”) and surreal images of longing and wind feel unexpected.
reasons.sadness: A caged, tattered bird in an unsuitable climate, distant gazes and a plea of ‘Enough’ evoke strong melancholy and pity.
reasons.anger: A quiet indignation at the animal’s confinement and mistreatment comes through, but it’s restrained rather than explosive.

A pocket watch had fallen behind a chest of drawers, where it went on ticking away all by itself.

A mouse found it and laughed.

"What a fool! Nobody's here to look at you—so why do you keep on running?"

"Because I keep running even when no one is looking," the pocket watch replied, "I am useful whenever someone does look."

"Anyone who works only when no one is watching, or only when someone is watching—either way, that's a thief."

The mouse flushed with shame and scurried off without another word.

reasons.interest: The dialogue between the mouse and pocket watch presents a clever philosophical exchange about purpose and duty that engages the reader intellectually. The personification and moral lesson make it entertaining and thought-provoking.
reasons.surprise: The pocket watch's response subverts expectations—rather than being defensive, it offers wisdom. The mouse's accusation that both types of work constitute theft is an unexpected moral twist that challenges conventional thinking.
reasons.sadness: There is a melancholic undertone to the pocket watch's lonely existence behind the chest of drawers, and the mouse's shame and silent departure convey a bittersweet moment of humbling realization.
reasons.anger: The mouse's initial mockery and accusatory tone toward the watch creates mild indignation, though the story resolves this gently rather than escalating conflict or injustice.

Text identifier: T1
Title: The Pocket Watch
Author: Kyusaku Yumeno
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
A pocket watch had fallen behind a chest of drawers, where it went on ticking away all by itself.

A mouse found it and laughed.

"What a fool! Nobody's here to look at you—so why do you keep on running?"

"Because I keep running even when no one is looking," the pocket watch replied, "I am useful whenever someone does look."

"Anyone who works only when no one is watching, or only when someone is watching—either way, that's a thief."

The mouse flushed with shame and scurried off without another word.

reasons.interest: The dialogue between mouse and watch presents a clever philosophical exchange about purpose and duty that engages the reader intellectually, with an unexpected moral lesson embedded in the interaction.
reasons.surprise: The mouse's accusation that the watch is a 'thief' for working regardless of observation is an unexpected twist that reframes the entire conversation and challenges conventional thinking about virtue.
reasons.sadness: The mouse's shame and silent departure conveys a melancholic moment of humiliation and self-realization, though tempered by the didactic nature of the fable.
reasons.anger: There is mild indignation in the mouse's initial mockery and the accusatory tone of calling the watch a thief, but the overall tone remains philosophical rather than wrathful.

Text identifier: T2
Title: The Money and the Pistol
Author: Kyusaku Yumeno
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
A burglar broke into a miser's house, brandished a pistol, and demanded money. The Miser said:

"I refuse to just hand over money for nothing. But if you'll sell me that pistol, I'll buy it for a thousand yen. Once you have my money, you won't be needing a pistol anyway."

The burglar thought it over for a while, and in the end sold the pistol to the Miser for a thousand yen. The moment the Miser had the pistol in his hands, he leveled it at the burglar as though ready to fire and bellowed:

"Now then—hand it all over! Not just that money, but everything else you've stolen! Hand it over, or I'll shoot you dead!"

The burglar doubled over laughing.

"Ha ha ha! That's a toy pistol, you fool—pull the trigger all you like, it won't fire a single bullet!"

And with that, he dashed out the front door. The Miser flung the pistol aside and chased after him, and the two were soon grappling in the street—until a policeman happened along and hauled them both off to the station.

When the police looked into the matter, they discovered that the thousand yen the burglar had received was counterfeit, every last bill. And the pistol, as it turned out, was real after all.

Both men were thrown in jail.

reasons.interest: The story is a clever, darkly comedic tale with witty dialogue and an ironic twist that makes it entertaining and intellectually engaging. The back-and-forth deception between characters creates an amusing narrative structure.
reasons.surprise: The multiple reversals are highly unexpected: the burglar sells the pistol, the miser attempts to turn the tables, the revelation that the pistol is real while the money is counterfeit, and both ending up in jail. Each twist subverts expectations effectively.
reasons.sadness: The ending carries a melancholic tone with both characters imprisoned as a result of their greed and deception, but the overall darkly comic framing prevents deep sadness from dominating the emotional response.
reasons.anger: The story evokes mild indignation at the mutual deception and foolishness of both characters, but the humorous tone and ironic justice temper any strong anger response.

"Ha ha ha! That's a toy pistol, you fool—pull the trigger all you like, it won't fire a single bullet!"

And with that, he dashed out the front door. The Miser flung the pistol aside and chased after him, and the two were soon grappling in the street—until a policeman happened along and hauled them both off to the station.

reasons.interest: The story presents a clever reversal of expectations with witty dialogue and an ironic twist that engages the reader's intellect. The back-and-forth deception between burglar and miser is entertaining and well-constructed.
reasons.surprise: The multiple layers of revelation—the toy pistol being real, the money being counterfeit, and both men ending up in jail—create genuine shock value. Each twist subverts what the reader and characters believe to be true.
reasons.sadness: The ending carries a melancholic tone with both characters imprisoned as a result of their greed and deception, suggesting a cautionary tale about moral compromise. However, the darkly comedic framing limits the emotional weight.
reasons.anger: The story evokes mild indignation at the mutual deception and the absurdity of both characters' schemes, but the tone remains more playful than genuinely outrage-inducing. The ironic justice delivered feels more satisfying than anger-provoking.

Text identifier: T2
Title: The Money and the Pistol
Author: Kyusaku Yumeno
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
A burglar broke into a miser's house, brandished a pistol, and demanded money. The Miser said:

"I refuse to just hand over money for nothing. But if you'll sell me that pistol, I'll buy it for a thousand yen. Once you have my money, you won't be needing a pistol anyway."

The burglar thought it over for a while, and in the end sold the pistol to the Miser for a thousand yen. The moment the Miser had the pistol in his hands, he leveled it at the burglar as though ready to fire and bellowed:

"Now then—hand it all over! Not just that money, but everything else you've stolen! Hand it over, or I'll shoot you dead!"

The burglar doubled over laughing.

"Ha ha ha! That's a toy pistol, you fool—pull the trigger all you like, it won't fire a single bullet!"

And with that, he dashed out the front door. The Miser flung the pistol aside and chased after him, and the two were soon grappling in the street—until a policeman happened along and hauled them both off to the station.

When the police looked into the matter, they discovered that the thousand yen the burglar had received was counterfeit, every last bill. And the pistol, as it turned out, was real after all.

Both men were thrown in jail.

reasons.interest: The story presents a clever battle of wits with escalating deceptions and reversals that engage the reader's intellect and sense of humor about the absurd situation.
reasons.surprise: The multiple plot twists—the miser's counter-scheme, the revelation that the pistol is real, the counterfeit money discovery—create genuine shock and subvert expectations throughout.
reasons.sadness: The ending carries melancholic weight as both characters face imprisonment despite their cunning, suggesting futility and the consequences of greed and crime.
reasons.anger: The miser's indignation at being outwitted and his aggressive response to the burglar convey frustration and displeasure, though tempered by the story's darkly comedic tone.

Text identifier: T3
Title: The Tattered Ostrich
Author: Kotaro Takamura
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
What pleasure can there be in keeping an ostrich?
In this muddy pen, barely four *tsubo* across, at a zoo—
aren't those legs far too long-strided?
Isn't that neck far too long?
In this country where snow falls, aren't those feathers far too tattered?
It eats hardtack, sure enough, because hunger compels it—
but look: aren't those eyes forever gazing into the distance?
Isn't it burning with an abandon beyond all caring for self or world?
Isn't it braced, even now, for the coming of a lapis-blue wind?
Isn't that small, unadorned head spinning with a dream beyond all horizons?
This is no longer an ostrich, can't you see?
O humankind—
enough. Enough of this.

reasons.interest: The poem's philosophical questioning and vivid imagery of the caged ostrich create intellectual engagement through its exploration of confinement versus freedom and the transformation of the animal into a symbol.
reasons.surprise: The repeated rhetorical questions and the unexpected shift from describing a zoo animal to a metaphysical meditation on transcendence and the plea to humankind provide revelatory moments.
reasons.sadness: The melancholic tone permeates the work—the ostrich's confinement in a muddy pen, its tattered feathers in snow, its yearning gaze into distance, and the speaker's weary plea 'enough' evoke profound poignancy about suffering and displacement.
reasons.anger: The underlying indignation about the ostrich's captivity and the implicit critique of human treatment of nature create a sense of displeasure, though tempered by the poem's contemplative rather than accusatory tone.

Text identifier: T1
Title: The Pocket Watch
Author: Kyusaku Yumeno
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
A pocket watch had fallen behind a chest of drawers, where it went on ticking away all by itself.

A mouse found it and laughed.

"What a fool! Nobody's here to look at you—so why do you keep on running?"

"Because I keep running even when no one is looking," the pocket watch replied, "I am useful whenever someone does look."

"Anyone who works only when no one is watching, or only when someone is watching—either way, that's a thief."

The mouse flushed with shame and scurried off without another word.

reasons.interest: The fable is witty and intellectually enjoyable, presenting a clever philosophical debate about work and integrity.
reasons.surprise: The mouse's unexpected accusation and its subsequent shame create a novel and revelatory twist on the common moral tale.
reasons.sadness: The text has a slight melancholic undertone in the watch's lonely, steadfast ticking, but it is not a primary focus.
reasons.anger: The text evokes minimal displeasure; the mouse's accusation might cause mild indignation, but it is quickly resolved by its shame.

Title: The Pocket Watch
Author: Kyusaku Yumeno
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
A pocket watch had fallen behind a chest of drawers, where it went on ticking away all by itself.

A mouse found it and laughed.

"What a fool! Nobody's here to look at you—so why do you keep on running?"

"Because I keep running even when no one is looking," the pocket watch replied, "I am useful whenever someone does look."

"Anyone who works only when no one is watching, or only when someone is watching—either way, that's a thief."

The mouse flushed with shame and scurried off without another word.

reasons.anger: The text evokes no indignation; the moral lesson is delivered without provoking displeasure.

Text identifier: T1
Title: The Pocket Watch
Author: Kyusaku Yumeno
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
A pocket watch had fallen behind a chest of drawers, where it went on ticking away all by itself.

A mouse found it and laughed.

"What a fool! Nobody's here to look at you—so why do you keep on running?"

"Because I keep running even when no one is looking," the pocket watch replied, "I am useful whenever someone does look."

"Anyone who works only when no one is watching, or only when someone is watching—either way, that's a thief."

The mouse flushed with shame and scurried off without another word.

reasons.interest: The dialogue is witty and allegorical, presenting an amusing philosophical debate about work and virtue.
reasons.surprise: The pocket watch's unexpected, principled retort reverses the mouse's initial mockery, creating a revelatory turn.
reasons.sadness: The tone is not melancholic; the exchange is didactic and light, ending with the mouse's retreat, not sorrow.
reasons.anger: No indignation is evoked; the mouse's shame is one of realization, not anger, and the watch's reply is calm.

Text identifier: T2
Title: The Money and the Pistol
Author: Kyusaku Yumeno
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
A burglar broke into a miser's house, brandished a pistol, and demanded money. The Miser said:

"I refuse to just hand over money for nothing. But if you'll sell me that pistol, I'll buy it for a thousand yen. Once you have my money, you won't be needing a pistol anyway."

The burglar thought it over for a while, and in the end sold the pistol to the Miser for a thousand yen. The moment the Miser had the pistol in his hands, he leveled it at the burglar as though ready to fire and bellowed:

"Now then—hand it all over! Not just that money, but everything else you've stolen! Hand it over, or I'll shoot you dead!"

The burglar doubled over laughing.

"Ha ha ha! That's a toy pistol, you fool—pull the trigger all you like, it won't fire a single bullet!"

And with that, he dashed out the front door. The Miser flung the pistol aside and chased after him, and the two were soon grappling in the street—until a policeman happened along and hauled them both off to the station.

When the police looked into the matter, they discovered that the thousand yen the burglar had received was counterfeit, every last bill. And the pistol, as it turned out, was real after all.

Both men were thrown in jail.

reasons.anger: The text's ironic twist and mutual deceit provoke mild frustration at the characters' foolishness, but no strong indignation.
reasons.interest: The clever, ironic twist and humorous reversal of expectations makes the story highly entertaining and intellectually enjoyable.
reasons.sadness: The story is a humorous farce with no tragic or poignant elements; the characters' fates feel like ironic justice, not sadness.
reasons.surprise: The double-twist ending—counterfeit money and a real pistol—is highly unexpected and revelatory.

Text identifier: T2
Title: The Money and the Pistol
Author: Kyusaku Yumeno
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
A burglar broke into a miser's house, brandished a pistol, and demanded money. The Miser said:

"I refuse to just hand over money for nothing. But if you'll sell me that pistol, I'll buy it for a thousand yen. Once you have my money, you won't be needing a pistol anyway."

The burglar thought it over for a while, and in the end sold the pistol to the Miser for a thousand yen. The moment the Miser had the pistol in his hands, he leveled it at the burglar as though ready to fire and bellowed:

"Now then—hand it all over! Not just that money, but everything else you've stolen! Hand it over, or I'll shoot you dead!"

The burglar doubled over laughing.

"Ha ha ha! That's a toy pistol, you fool—pull the trigger all you like, it won't fire a single bullet!"

And with that, he dashed out the front door. The Miser flung the pistol aside and chased after him, and the two were soon grappling in the street—until a policeman happened along and hauled them both off to the station.

When the police looked into the matter, they discovered that the thousand yen the burglar had received was counterfeit, every last bill. And the pistol, as it turned out, was real after all.

Both men were thrown in jail.

reasons.interest: The story is highly entertaining due to its ironic twists and the clever, deceitful exchange between the miser and the burglar, which creates a humorous and intellectually engaging narrative.
reasons.surprise: The text feels highly unexpected and revelatory because of the double twist ending: the money is counterfeit and the pistol is real, subverting the reader's assumptions from the earlier reveal.
reasons.sadness: The story is not sad or melancholic; it is a darkly comedic tale of mutual deceit where both characters face consequences, but there is little emotional weight or poignancy to evoke sadness.
reasons.anger: The text evokes minimal anger or indignation; the characters are both criminals engaged in trickery, so their mutual downfall feels more like ironic justice than a cause for reader displeasure.

Text identifier: T2
Title: The Money and the Pistol
Author: Kyusaku Yumeno
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
A burglar broke into a miser's house, brandished a pistol, and demanded money. The Miser said:

"I refuse to just hand over money for nothing. But if you'll sell me that pistol, I'll buy it for a thousand yen. Once you have my money, you won't be needing a pistol anyway."

The burglar thought it over for a while, and in the end sold the pistol to the Miser for a thousand yen. The moment the Miser had the pistol in his hands, he leveled it at the burglar as though ready to fire and bellowed:

"Now then—hand it all over! Not just that money, but everything else you've stolen! Hand it over, or I'll shoot you dead!"

The burglar doubled over laughing.

"Ha ha ha! That's a toy pistol, you fool—pull the trigger all you like, it won't fire a single bullet!"

And with that, he dashed out the front door. The Miser flung the pistol aside and chased after him, and the two were soon grappling in the street—until a policeman happened along and hauled them both off to the station.

When the police looked into the matter, they discovered that the thousand yen the burglar had received was counterfeit, every last bill. And the pistol, as it turned out, was real after all.

Both men were thrown in jail.

reasons.interest: The story is highly entertaining due to its clever plot twists, ironic humor, and the witty dialogue between the miser and the burglar.
reasons.surprise: The narrative contains multiple unexpected revelations: the pistol is a toy, the money is counterfeit, and the pistol is real after all, creating a series of novel twists.
reasons.sadness: The text is not melancholic; it is a humorous, ironic tale of mutual deception with a neutral, matter-of-fact ending about both going to jail.
reasons.anger: The central character, the miser, likely feels indignation and displeasure at being robbed, tricked about the pistol, and ultimately arrested, though his primary drive is cunning rather than rage.

Text identifier: T3
Title: The Tattered Ostrich
Author: Kotaro Takamura
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
What pleasure can there be in keeping an ostrich?
In this muddy pen, barely four *tsubo* across, at a zoo—
aren't those legs far too long-strided?
Isn't that neck far too long?
In this country where snow falls, aren't those feathers far too tattered?
It eats hardtack, sure enough, because hunger compels it—
but look: aren't those eyes forever gazing into the distance?
Isn't it burning with an abandon beyond all caring for self or world?
Isn't it braced, even now, for the coming of a lapis-blue wind?
Isn't that small, unadorned head spinning with a dream beyond all horizons?
This is no longer an ostrich, can't you see?
O humankind—
enough. Enough of this.

reasons.anger: The text's direct address ('O humankind— / enough. Enough of this.') and depiction of cruel confinement evoke indignation at the human treatment of the animal.
reasons.interest: The poem is intellectually engaging, using vivid imagery and rhetorical questions to explore confinement, dignity, and longing, prompting reflection.
reasons.sadness: The imagery of the tattered, confined ostrich gazing longingly into the distance evokes a strong sense of melancholy and poignancy.
reasons.surprise: The shift from observation to a revelatory, accusatory address ('This is no longer an ostrich... O humankind') provides an unexpected and novel turn.

Text identifier: T3
Title: The Tattered Ostrich
Author: Kotaro Takamura
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
What pleasure can there be in keeping an ostrich?
In this muddy pen, barely four *tsubo* across, at a zoo—
aren't those legs far too long-strided?
Isn't that neck far too long?
In this country where snow falls, aren't those feathers far too tattered?
It eats hardtack, sure enough, because hunger compels it—
but look: aren't those eyes forever gazing into the distance?
Isn't it burning with an abandon beyond all caring for self or world?
Isn't it braced, even now, for the coming of a lapis-blue wind?
Isn't that small, unadorned head spinning with a dream beyond all horizons?
This is no longer an ostrich, can't you see?
O humankind—
enough. Enough of this.

reasons.interest: The poem is intellectually engaging in its stark observation and philosophical questioning of captivity.
reasons.surprise: The shift from physical description to a declaration of transcendent, wind-borne defiance is unexpected and revelatory.
reasons.sadness: The imagery of the confined, tattered bird forced to eat hardtack evokes a strong sense of melancholy and pity.
reasons.anger: The final, accusatory address to humankind evokes indignation at the cruelty of the confinement it depicts.

Text identifier: T1
Title: The Pocket Watch
Author: Kyusaku Yumeno
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
A pocket watch had fallen behind a chest of drawers, where it went on ticking away all by itself.

A mouse found it and laughed.

"What a fool! Nobody's here to look at you—so why do you keep on running?"

"Because I keep running even when no one is looking," the pocket watch replied, "I am useful whenever someone does look."

"Anyone who works only when no one is watching, or only when someone is watching—either way, that's a thief."

The mouse flushed with shame and scurried off without another word.

interest.reason: The concise fable structure and dialogue on intrinsic purpose make it intellectually engaging and quietly entertaining.

surprise.reason: While the moral is familiar, the mouse's abrupt shame and the watch's calm rebuttal offer a mild, satisfying revelation.

sadness.reason: The image of the unseen, ticking watch leaves a faint, poignant aftertaste of quiet isolation, though the tone remains instructive.

anger.reason: The swift moral resolution and the watch's steady wisdom prevent any lingering indignation or displeasure.

Text identifier: T1
Title: The Pocket Watch
Author: Kyusaku Yumeno
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
A pocket watch had fallen behind a chest of drawers, where it went on ticking away all by itself.

A mouse found it and laughed.

"What a fool! Nobody's here to look at you—so why do you keep on running?"

"Because I keep running even when no one is looking," the pocket watch replied, "I am useful whenever someone does look."

"Anyone who works only when no one is watching, or only when someone is watching—either way, that's a thief."

The mouse flushed with shame and scurried off without another word.

reasons.sadness: The solitary ticking behind furniture suggests quiet isolation, though the character accepts it with quiet resolve.
reasons.anger: The watch remains entirely unprovoked, responding with calm principle rather than indignation.

Text identifier: T2
Title: The Money and the Pistol
Author: Kyusaku Yumeno
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
A burglar broke into a miser's house, brandished a pistol, and demanded money. The Miser said:

"I refuse to just hand over money for nothing. But if you'll sell me that pistol, I'll buy it for a thousand yen. Once you have my money, you won't be needing a pistol anyway."

The burglar thought it over for a while, and in the end sold the pistol to the Miser for a thousand yen. The moment the Miser had the pistol in his hands, he leveled it at the burglar as though ready to fire and bellowed:

"Now then—hand it all over! Not just that money, but everything else you've stolen! Hand it over, or I'll shoot you dead!"

The burglar doubled over laughing.

"Ha ha ha! That's a toy pistol, you fool—pull the trigger all you like, it won't fire a single bullet!"

And with that, he dashed out the front door. The Miser flung the pistol aside and chased after him, and the two were soon grappling in the street—until a policeman happened along and hauled them both off to the station.

When the police looked into the matter, they discovered that the thousand yen the burglar had received was counterfeit, every last bill. And the pistol, as it turned out, was real after all.

Both men were thrown in jail.

parsed.reason_interest: The clever dialogue and rapid situational reversals make the fable highly entertaining and intellectually engaging.
parsed.reason_surprise: The closing twist revealing the money as counterfeit and the pistol as real sharply subverts the reader's expectations.
parsed.reason_sadness: The story's satirical, farcical tone prioritizes irony over emotional depth, leaving little room for melancholy.
parsed.reason_anger: The characters' mutual greed and foolishness lead to a comically deserved arrest, evoking amusement rather than indignation.

Language of this prompt: en

Text:
A burglar broke into a miser's house, brandished a pistol, and demanded money. The Miser said:

"I refuse to just hand over money for nothing. But if you'll sell me that pistol, I'll buy it for a thousand yen. Once you have my money, you won't be needing a pistol anyway."

The burglar thought it over for a while, and in the end sold the pistol to the Miser for a thousand yen. The moment the Miser had the pistol in his hands, he leveled it at the burglar as though ready to fire and bellowed:

"Now then—hand it all over! Not just that money, but everything else you've stolen! Hand it over, or I'll shoot you dead!"

The burglar doubled over laughing.

"Ha ha ha! That's a toy pistol, you fool—pull the trigger all you like, it won't fire a single bullet!"

And with that, he dashed out the front door. The Miser flung the pistol aside and chased after him, and the two were soon grappling in the street—until a policeman happened along and hauled them both off to the station.

When the police looked into the matter, they discovered that the thousand yen the burglar had received was counterfeit, every last bill. And the pistol, as it turned out, was real after all.

Both men were thrown in jail.

parsed.interest_reason: The witty dialogue and clever role-reversal create an intellectually entertaining and fast-paced narrative.
parsed.surprise_reason: The final revelation that the money was counterfeit while the pistol was actually real delivers a sharp, unexpected double twist.
parsed.sadness_reason: The purely satirical and comedic tone, ending with mutual folly rather than loss, leaves no room for melancholy.
parsed.anger_reason: The detached, fable-like presentation of greed and foolishness reads as dark comedy rather than a provocation of moral outrage.

Text identifier: T3
Title: The Tattered Ostrich
Author: Kotaro Takamura
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
What pleasure can there be in keeping an ostrich?
In this muddy pen, barely four *tsubo* across, at a zoo—

Text identifier: T3
Title: The Tattered Ostrich
Author: Kotaro Takamura
Language of this prompt: en

Text:
What pleasure can there be in keeping an ostrich?
In this muddy pen, barely four *tsubo* across, at a zoo—
aren't those legs far too long-strided?
Isn't that neck far too long?
In this country where snow falls, aren't those feathers far too tattered?
It eats hardtack, sure enough, because hunger compels it—
but look: aren't those eyes forever gazing into the distance?
Isn't it burning with an abandon beyond all caring for self or world?
Isn't it braced, even now, for the coming of a lapis-blue wind?
Isn't that small, unadorned head spinning with a dream beyond all horizons?
This is no longer an ostrich, can't you see?
O humankind—
enough. Enough of this.

interest.reason: The speaker’s probing rhetorical questions and sustained contemplation of the bird’s inner life reflect strong intellectual engagement.

surprise.reason: The gradual realization that the captive bird harbors a transcendent, horizon-spanning spirit provides a moderate sense of revelation.

sadness.reason: The vivid imagery of the cramped, muddy pen, harsh snow, and tattered feathers evokes profound melancholy for the creature’s suffering.

anger.reason: The direct, exasperated address to humankind and the repeated command to stop convey strong moral indignation at human captivity.
